evidences.title: The Fragile U.S.-Iran Ceasefire: Issues to Watch
evidences.content: In the end, the ceasefire is less a resolution than a pause in a conflict whose underlying drivers remain not only intact but, in some cases, intensified. The nuclear issue is unresolved, Lebanon is destabilized, the risk of terrorism persists, and U.S. alliances have been strained—all while Israel and Iran retain strong incentives to continue a shadow war that periodically erupts into open violence. Even if large-scale fighting does not immediately resume, the United States faces a region marked by persistent instability, emboldened adversaries, wary allies, and a continuing cycle of escalation that will be difficult to control or conclude. [...] It is possible that the ceasefire itself will be the settlement: The United States, Israel, and Iran will not come to a final deal, but the ceasefire will continue indefinitely, with the risk of a flare-up hovering over the region. #### Iran’s Nuclear Program Iran’s nuclear program has been at the center of the U.S.-Iran conflict for decades, and over the years the United States has tried sanctions, cyberattacks, negotiations, and limited military force to stop it. If negotiations falter, Washington faces a choice: accept an inconclusive outcome or recommit to a decisive campaign. In this scenario, the United States chooses recommitment: the systematic destruction of Iran’s distributed drone and missile networks, the aggressive pursuit and seizure of nuclear materials, a full-court military offensive to clear the coastline abutting the strait, and pressure sufficient to produce regime collapse or capitulation. [...] If the Hormuz disruption remains contained—absorbed through coordinated strategic reserve releases and alternative Gulf energy routing—the system holds. US-Gulf security partnerships deepen. The energy shock is painful but finite. The outcome is marginal: a modest erosion of US alliances, especially in Europe, offset by a demonstrable, if incomplete, use of force. China gains incrementally, at least in the sense that its strategic partner survived the US-Israeli onslaught. evidences.title: Experts react: The US and Iran just announced an interim ...
evidences.content: The major downside cost has been Iran’s threats to the Strait of Hormuz and the US retaliatory blockade,which have choked off global energy flows, leading to higher energy prices and a global economic shock. The deal announced this weekend promises to alleviate this economic pressure. I suspect it will hold because both sides have a strong economic incentive to get energy flowing to global markets. Trump may succeed, therefore, in defanging one of the world’s most dangerous terrorist states in exchange for several months of higher energy prices. That is a good trade. [...] While the deal will reportedly reopen the Strait of Hormuz, Iran has demonstrated that its longstanding threats to close the strait are not just bluster and it can wreak havoc on the global economy. The ability to close the strait is a potent weapon that Iran will threaten to wield in the future. There is a possibility that this ceasefire could pave the way for a more permanent deal, but it is more likely this will be a temporary and fragile understanding that will, in the best-case scenario, prevent renewed war through the end of this administration. _—Victoria J. evidences.title: Iran's Fragile Ceasefire | Model Diplomat
evidences.content: The current ceasefire reflects an uneasy balance—an unsteady truce caught between the potential for diplomatic thaw and the risk of military escalation. ## What to watch next The ceasefire's scheduled expiration is just days away. Watch for either its extension or collapse, which will signal whether diplomatic channels are holding or pressure is building for military action. Parallelly, follow the US-Iran JCPOA talks for signs of progress or breakdown. Terms that address sanctions relief, uranium limits, and regional security are the key sticking points. Also, monitor Israeli military activity and Iran’s proxy responses, which could either provoke or deter new escalations. [...] The ceasefire in Iran’s northwest represents a microcosm of larger geopolitical currents. Israeli strikes in the region have intensified recently, aiming to curb Iran’s support for proxies in Lebanon, Syria, and Gaza, but at the cost of inflaming tensions on multiple fronts. Iran’s own responses—whether military or diplomatic—are closely watched as indicators of broader shifts. ## Why this matters: a test of diplomacy and regional stability Iran’s internal mood is one of cautious pessimism.
